Gap timing inside modal westbound transit envelope; OSM Overpass chokepoint geometry (200 maritime features) consistent with predicted corridor.

reads.summary: 21 cached DANTI coordinate hits are inside the Hormuz source window, but they are point observations, not continuous tracks. IEA's Hormuz geometry (29 nm wide, two 2-mile lanes) makes coordinate density operationally meaningful while preserving the stale-cache alternative.

OFAC's Sanctions List Service is the authoritative SDN source for blocked maritime vessels; Treasury's Adrian Darya 1 action provides the Iran-linked tanker precedent. The read supports entity-risk enrichment, not behavior attribution.

Source chain is usable but bounded: DANTI coordinates, OFAC/OpenSanctions joins, and cached normalized OSINT agree at the entity-risk layer. No kinematic continuity or intent indicator is promoted from these rows.
Insufficient evidence to infer hostile intent. Sanctions presence and cached coordinates create a review case, but no independent INTENT_INDICATOR is present. Guard Layer 2 routes to collection.

Recommend SAR/RF corroboration on the top watchlist hulls. MARAD 2026-004 describes high regional risk and degraded positional integrity, so the next useful act is independent position confirmation rather than passive monitoring.
No direct visual ID is asserted from the cached rows. Visual contributes tasking geometry: confirm high-priority hull presence with SAR or EO before any behavior claim is made.

Qeshm and Bandar Abbas clusters are density signals, not track continuity. IEA and EIA chokepoint baselines make density useful for prioritization, while the normal-congestion hypothesis stays preserved.
Identity remains cluster-level. The case names vessels inside the cluster only as source rows; it does not collapse them into a single coordinated actor or infer cargo ownership.

AIS density near anchorages can be distorted by reception and duplicate rows. GFW's AIS-gap caveats and MARAD GNSS warnings keep source quality explicit, but the cluster evidence is still useful for collection prioritization.

Insufficient evidence to infer intent. Loitering or anchorage density can be normal port staging in a critical chokepoint; without an independent intent indicator, the guard refuses escalation language.
Recommend SAR revisit over the two cluster centers and RF sampling for active transmitters. The useful next question is whether the same hulls persist across independent sensors.
Visual read is collection-oriented: SAR can count hulls and test cluster persistence, but the cached row alone cannot identify individual vessel behavior.

The signal is routing metadata, not motion. Six cached rows with Iranian last-port values open a review case, but the kinematic specialist refuses to infer cargo flow without a track segment or port-event chain.

Foreign flag plus Iranian last_port is sanctions-laundering relevant under the OFAC maritime-evasion advisory, but it remains an identity/routing cue. Cargo origin and beneficial ownership are not asserted.
The source chain is adequate for a review signal but still cached-only. Last-port fields can lag reality, so stale metadata remains an explicit alternative hypothesis.
Insufficient evidence to infer sanctions-evasion intent. OFAC context makes the pattern relevant, but the case lacks cargo documents, STS evidence, or an independent intent indicator.
Recommend registry, port-call, and SAR/RF corroboration on the six named vessels. The collection question is whether last-port metadata matches independent port events and hull movement.
Visual tasking can verify hull presence near declared port-call windows, but it cannot prove cargo origin. This read stays at the identity/routing layer.

Destination strings are routing metadata, not movement. EIA's Asia-flow baseline makes China-routing operationally relevant, but FOR ORDER / TO ORDER strings require track and port-event corroboration.
The identity read treats repeated destination strings as a vessel-selection cue, not a sanctions hit. Vessel-level de-duplication and registry joins are still required before ownership claims.
Signal integrity is bounded by duplicate cached rows and free-text destinations. The pattern is useful as a triage layer but cannot resolve cargo, owner, or intent without independent evidence.
Insufficient evidence to infer evasion intent. EIA's Asia-flow data and OFAC's sanctions-evasion advisory make the pattern relevant, but destination strings are not an intent indicator.
Recommend de-duplicated vessel list, port-event reconstruction, and SAR/RF on HAMOUNA and DARYABAR. The aim is to turn free-text routing hints into independent custody evidence.
Visual contributes only after the routing list is de-duplicated. EO/SAR can verify hull presence near claimed route points but cannot read a destination string as cargo proof.
A cached 31 kt speed is physically suspect for this vessel class, but the kinematic read cannot choose between spoofing, parser artifact, or a rare operational exception without adjacent positions.
Identity is held but not resolved. The signal is about the quality of the AIS row, not whether ROSHAK is the same hull across a dark gap.

Source chain contested. MARAD states AIS is open, unencrypted, and spoofable; IMO says AIS-equipped ships should keep AIS operating except for protected navigation. A single implausible AIS row is enough to refuse downstream movement or intent claims.
Insufficient evidence to infer intent. The upstream Signal Integrity refusal blocks intent inference; the only supported conclusion is AIS/data-quality review.
Recommend AIS integrity review, adjacent-position reconstruction, and RF/SAR corroboration. Collection should determine whether the 31 kt value is spoofing, parser error, or a transient maneuver.
Visual is a corroboration target only. SAR/EO can test whether a hull exists near the asserted position, but the cached AIS row alone remains contested.
